toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

data.reasoningText:
The user wants to enable hot reload for their .NET test project. With `dotnet test`, hot reload is enabled via `dotnet test --hotreload` or by setting the `DOTNET_WATCH` or `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED` environment variable... Actually, let me think about this more carefully.

For .NET testing with the new Microsoft Testing Platform (which they're using since `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport` is true), hot reload can be enabled via:

1. Environment variable `TESTINGPLATFORM_HOTRELOAD_ENABLED=1` 
2. Or by using `dotnet test --hotreload`


3. But since they don't want to set this manually each time, I should look at configuring it persistently through a `launchSettings.json`, `Directory.Build.props`, or `.env` file approach. Setting MSBuild properties... Let me think through the most reliable approaches here. Setting `<TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>--hotreload</TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments>` in the csproj should pass the flag automatically when running `dotnet test`, though I'm not entirely certain that property name is correct. The safest bet is probably using a `.runsettings` file with environment variables, which I know works reliably. the Microsoft Testing Platform supports setting hot reload through MSBuild properties in the csproj file. I'm trying to pin down the exact property name—it's either `TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled` or `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` with the `--hotreload` flag, and I want to make sure I recommend the right one. Setting environment variables through MSBuild properties might not work reliably with `dotnet test`, and a `.runsettings` file could have timing issues since the hot reload variable needs to be set before the test host starts. The most straightforward approach is probably just adding `--hotreload` as a default argument through MSBuild properties instead of trying to manage environment variables. The `.runsettings` file approach is the most reliable way to configure this—I can set the hot reload environment variable there and reference it in the project file so it applies automatically during test runs. The proper way to enable hot reload by default is through the `<TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>true</TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>` MSBuild property in the project file, though I should verify this against the actual documentation since the configuration can also be set via environment variables or configuration files depending on the testing platform setup. `TestingPlatformCommandLineArguments` might not be the right property name, so let me think through the most reliable approaches. I could set an environment variable through the project file, but that's tricky. The standard .NET approach would be using a `.runsettings` file, which is the conventional way to configure test execution settings.

But wait—for hot reload specifically, the environment variable needs to be set for the `dotnet test` command itself, not just the test host process. A `.runsettings` file only affects the test host, so that won't work here. I need to step back and figure out what `dotnet test --hotreload` actually does—it's a feature of the `dotnet test` command that watches for file changes and reruns tests with hot reload enabled, which means I need to configure this at the command level rather than through test settings.

Looking at this more carefully with the user's setup—.NET 9.0, MSTest 3.8.0, and the Microsoft Testing Platform enabled—the `dotnet test` command uses the Microsoft Testing Platform, which supports hot reload configuration. I can enable this either through command-line arguments or through an MSBuild property like `<TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>true</TestingPlatformHotReloadEnabled>` in the project file.

For running from the command line without typing flags each time, the most straightforward approach is either setting an environment variable that the platform respects, creating a shell alias, or configuring it in the project file itself so it's always enabled by default. I'm trying to figure out the right MSBuild property to enable hot reload by default in the test project. The candidates are `<EnableHotReload>true</EnableHotReload>` or `<TestingPlatformHotReload>true</TestingPlatformHotReload>`, but I'm not entirely certain which one the Microsoft Testing Platform actually respects, so I need to verify the correct property name. I'm also considering whether `dotnet watch test` would be simpler—it watches for changes and reruns tests, though that's different from the `--hotreload` flag which applies changes without a full rebuild. The `--hotreload` approach seems more aligned with what the user is asking for, so I should verify what the SDK actually supports by checking the help output and testing it directly.

arguments.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-5a8a9a8c173e4d5faeab02905447ce6a && dotnet test --help 2>&1 | head -60", "description": "Check dotnet test help for hot reload options", "initial_wait": 15}

toolArgs.description: Check dotnet test help for hot reload options

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
Description:
  .NET Test Driver

Usage:
  dotnet test [options] [[--] <additional arguments>...]]

Options:
  -s, --settings <SETTINGS_FILE>       The settings file to use when running tests.
  -t, --list-tests                     List the discovered tests instead of running the tests.
  -e, --environment <NAME="VALUE">     Sets the value of an environment variable. 
                                       Creates the variable if it does not exist, overrides if it does. 
                                       This will force the tests to be run in an isolated process. 
                                       This argument can be specified multiple times to provide multiple variables.
  
                                       Examples:
                                       -e VARIABLE=abc
                                       -e VARIABLE="value with spaces"
                                       -e VARIABLE="value;seperated with;semicolons"
                                       -e VAR1=abc -e VAR2=def -e VAR3=ghi
  --filter <EXPRESSION>                Run tests that match the given expression.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Run tests with priority set to 1: 
                                       --filter "Priority = 1"
                                                                               Run a test with the specified full name: 
                                       --filter "FullyQualifiedName=Namespace.ClassName.MethodName"
                                                                               Run tests that contain the specified 
                                       name: --filter "FullyQualifiedName~Namespace.Class"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-filtering for 
                                       more information on filtering support.
  --test-adapter-path <ADAPTER_PATH>   The path to the custom adapters to use for the test run.
  -l, --logger <LOGGER>                The logger to use for test results.
                                                                               Examples:
                                                                               Log in trx format using a unique file 
                                       name: --logger trx
                                                                               Log in trx format using the specified 
                                       file name: --logger "trx;LogFileName=<TestResults.trx>"
                                                                               See https://aka.ms/vstest-report for 
                                       more information on logger arguments.
  -o, --output <OUTPUT_DIR>            The output directory to place built artifacts in.
  --artifacts-path <ARTIFACTS_DIR>     The artifacts path. All output from the project, including build, publish, and 
                                       pack output, will go in subfolders under the specified path.
  -d, --diag <LOG_FILE>                Enable verbose logging to the specified file.
  --no-build                           Do not build the project before testing. Implies --no-restore.
  --results-directory <RESULTS_DIR>    The directory where the test results will be placed.
                                       The specified directory will be created if it does not exist.
  --collect <DATA_COLLECTOR_NAME>      The friendly name of the data collector to use for the test run.
                                                                               More info here: 
                                       https://aka.ms/vstest-collect
  --blame                              Runs the tests in blame mode. This option is helpful in isolating problematic 
                                       tests that cause the test host to crash or hang, but it does not create a memory 
                                       dump by default. 
  
                                       When a crash is detected, it creates an sequence file in 
                                       TestResults/guid/guid_Sequence.xml that captures the order of tests that were 
                                       run before the crash.
  
                                       Based on the additional settings, hang dump or crash dump can also be collected.
  
                                       Example: 
                                         Timeout the test run when test takes more than the default timeout of 1 hour,
<exited with exit code 0>

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
the usage of Procdump can be forced by setting the VSTEST_DUMP_FORCEPROCDUMP 
                                       environment variable to 1.
  
                                       Implies --blame.
  --blame-crash-dump-type <DUMP_TYPE>  The type of crash dump to be collected. Supported values are full (default) and 
                                       mini. Implies --blame-crash.
  --blame-crash-collect-always         Enables collecting crash dump on expected as well as unexpected testhost exit.
  --blame-hang                         Run the tests in blame mode and enables collecting hang dump when test exceeds 
                                       the given timeout. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-dump-type <DUMP_TYPE>   The type of crash dump to be collected. The supported values are full (default), 
                                       mini, and none. When 'none' is used then test host is terminated on timeout, but 
                                       no dump is collected. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-timeout <TIMESPAN>      Per-test timeout, after which hang dump is triggered and the testhost process is 
                                       terminated. Default is 1h.
                                       The timeout value is specified in the following format: 1.5h / 90m / 5400s / 
                                       5400000ms. When no unit is used (e.g. 5400000), the value is assumed to be in 
                                       milliseconds.
                                       When used together with data driven tests, the timeout behavior depends on the 
                                       test adapter used. For xUnit, NUnit and MSTest 2.2.4+ the timeout is renewed 
                                       after every test case,
                                       For MSTest before 2.2.4, the timeout is used for all testcases.
  --nologo                             Run test(s), without displaying Microsoft Testplatform banner
  -c, --configuration <CONFIGURATION>  The configuration to use for running tests. The default for most projects is 
                                       'Debug'.
  -f, --framework <FRAMEWORK>          The target framework to run tests for. The target framework must also be 
                                       specified in the project file.
  -r, --runtime <RUNTIME_IDENTIFIER>   The target runtime to test for.
  --no-restore                         Do not restore the project before building.
  --interactive                        Allows the command to stop and wait for user input or action (for example to 
                                       complete authentication).
  -v, --verbosity <LEVEL>              Set the MSBuild verbosity level. Allowed values are q[uiet], m[inimal], 
                                       n[ormal], d[etailed], and diag[nostic].
  -a, --arch <ARCH>                    The target architecture.
  --os <OS>                            The target operating system.
  --disable-build-servers              Force the command to ignore any persistent build servers.
  -?, -h, --help                       Show command line help.

Additional Arguments:
  Arguments passed to the application that is being run.
<exited with exit code 0>

                                       no dump is collected. Implies --blame-hang.
  --blame-hang-timeout <TIMESPAN>      Per-test timeout, after which hang dump is triggered and the testhost process is 
                                       terminated. Default is 1h.
                                       The timeout value is specified in the following format: 1.5h / 90m / 5400s / 
                                       5400000ms. When no unit is used (e.g. 5400000), the value is assumed to be in 
                                       milliseconds.
                                       When used together with data driven tests, the timeout behavior depends on the 
                                       test adapter used. For xUnit, NUnit and MSTest 2.2.4+ the timeout is renewed 
                                       after every test case,
                                       For MSTest before 2.2.4, the timeout is used for all testcases.
  --nologo                             Run test(s), without displaying Microsoft Testplatform banner
  -c, --configuration <CONFIGURATION>  The configuration to use for running tests. The default for most projects is 
                                       'Debug'.
  -f, --framework <FRAMEWORK>          The target framework to run tests for. The target framework must also be 
                                       specified in the project file.
  -r, --runtime <RUNTIME_IDENTIFIER>   The target runtime to test for.
  --no-restore                         Do not restore the project before building.
  --interactive                        Allows the command to stop and wait for user input or action (for example to 
                                       complete authentication).
  -v, --verbosity <LEVEL>              Set the MSBuild verbosity level. Allowed values are q[uiet], m[inimal], 
                                       n[ormal], d[etailed], and diag[nostic].
  -a, --arch <ARCH>                    The target architecture.
  --os <OS>                            The target operating system.
  --disable-build-servers              Force the command to ignore any persistent build servers.
  -?, -h, --help                       Show command line help.

Additional Arguments:
  Arguments passed to the application that is being run.

data.reasoningText:
The `dotnet test --help` doesn't mention `--hotreload`. Let me check if the testing platform has its own help with the `--hotreload` option. Since they have `TestingPlatformDotnetTestSupport=true`, the testing platform might have additional options that are passed through as additional arguments.

Let me check `dotnet watch test` or the testing platform's own help.

Actually, I think the hot reload feature for tests in .NET 9 with the Microsoft Testing Platform might be accessed via `dotnet test -- --hotreload` (passing it as an additional argument to the testing platform), or it might just not be available in this version.


Let me see if the compiled test executable itself recognizes the `--hotreload` flag.

arguments.description: Search SDK files for hot reload properties

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Search SDK files for hot reload properties

data.reasoningText:
The build has errors - missing `using System;`. But that's a pre-existing issue, not related to my task. Let me focus on the hot reload configuration.

Since the build fails, I can't see the testing platform help. Let me try a different approach - check the .NET SDK docs or look at what MSBuild properties are available.

Actually, let me first check if `dotnet watch test` is what the user wants - that's the standard way to enable "hot reload" for tests. `dotnet watch` watches for file changes and re-runs/hot-reloads.

To always enable hot reload when running tests from the command line, the user needs to configure `dotnet watch` settings. The `launchSettings.json` is one way, but for tests, the typical approach is:


The key distinction here is that `dotnet watch test` automatically re-runs tests when files change, but I need to clarify what the user actually means by "hot reload" - they might want tests to re-execute automatically on code changes, or they might be looking for a project-level configuration that applies even when running `dotnet test` directly. I should focus on what the user actually needs rather than getting sidetracked by the build issues. For `dotnet watch test`, the configuration is typically controlled through project file properties that determine what files to watch and how to handle reloads.

The most straightforward approach would be to add a property to the csproj file that enables hot reload for the test runner, though I should verify that this property actually exists in the .NET SDK before recommending it.

arguments.description: Search SDK files for hot reload properties

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "find /usr/share/dotnet/sdk -name \"*.props\" -o -name \"*.targets\" 2>/dev/null | head -5 && grep -rl -i \"hotreload\\|hot.reload\" /usr/share/dotnet/sdk/9.0.312/Sdks/Microsoft.NET.Sdk/ 2>/dev/null | head -10", "description": "Search SDK files for hot reload properties", "initial_wait": 15}

data.reasoningText:
The SDK has `SupportsHotReload` capability but that's about whether the project supports hot reload, not about enabling it by default.

Let me search for testing platform hot reload configuration specifically. Let me check the NuGet packages installed for the project.
